claims.text: Mainstream cosmology has a real gap: the gravitational pull of visible matter is insufficient to explain how galaxies hold together at observed rotational speeds, and 'dark matter' — never directly detected — is essentially a Band-Aid hypothesis to cover this discrepancy.

claims.rationale: The galaxy-rotation problem (galaxies rotate too fast for visible mass alone) is a real and unresolved puzzle that motivates dark matter. However, calling dark matter 'just a Band-Aid' undersells the multiple independent lines of evidence (CMB acoustic peaks, gravitational lensing, large-scale structure, Bullet Cluster) that all point to the same ~5:1 dark:visible mass ratio. Dark matter has not been directly detected in lab experiments — that part is true — but it is far from a single-purpose patch.

claims.rationale: The 'plasma is the fourth state of matter and dominates visible matter in the universe' point is true (stars are plasma; the interstellar/intergalactic medium contains ionized gas). The Electric Universe theory's specific claim — that galaxy-spanning electric currents replace gravity as the binding force, and propagate FTL — is rejected by mainstream plasma physics and astrophysics. There is no observational evidence of such currents at the predicted scale, and FTL propagation contradicts experimentally well-tested relativity.

claims.text: Electric Universe proponents argue that planetary surface features like Earth's Grand Canyon and Mars' Valles Marineris were carved not by water erosion or tectonics but by interplanetary lightning bolts, citing the river-network branching patterns that resemble electrical circuit junctions.

claims.rationale: The dendritic-branching argument is fractal-pattern-matching and proves nothing on its own — branching networks emerge from many physical processes (river erosion, lightning, blood vessels, mycelium) because they are all energy-flow optimizations. Grand Canyon dating and stratigraphy strongly support water erosion over millions of years; Valles Marineris is a true tectonic rift with associated faulting visible in MOLA topography. Neither was carved by lightning; the EU explanation has no supporting field evidence.

claims.rationale: Velikovsky did publish 'Worlds in Collision' in 1950 and did use comparative mythology in the way described. The Saturn-as-pole-star, Kronos-as-Helios, and Latin-Saturn-as-Sol etymological points are real Velikovsky/Talbott observations. However, the inferential leap — that these myths encode a literal binary planet system — is rejected by mainstream celestial mechanics, which would not permit a Saturn-Earth binary at observable historical timescales without leaving overwhelming geological/orbital evidence. The mythological observations are real; the cosmic-history conclusion is not supported.

claims.text: David Talbott extended Velikovsky's work by cataloging a 'cosmic wheel' motif — three aligned celestial objects (Saturn far, Mars near-dark, Venus glowing center) — that recurs in prehistoric rock art across Ireland, California, Africa, Scandinavia, the Mediterranean, Mexico, and Mesopotamia, suggesting a shared real ancient sky-experience.

claims.rationale: Talbott's 'cosmic wheel' / 'polar configuration' is a real iconographic argument he and Wal Thornhill have promoted for decades. Cross-cultural concentric-circle and wheel motifs are widespread but generally interpreted by mainstream archaeologists as representing the sun, the year, the four directions, or local cosmology — not literal sky configurations. The convergence is real but underdetermined; the inference to a distinct ancient planetary alignment requires ignoring obvious alternative interpretations.

dependencies.rationale: Talbott's cosmic-wheel inventory is the iconographic continuation of Velikovsky's mythology-based argument.

claims.rationale: Anthony Peratt is a real plasma physicist (formerly at Los Alamos) who published 'Characteristics for the Occurrence of a High-Current Z-Pinch Aurora' (2003, IEEE Trans. Plasma Sci.) proposing the squatter-man-as-Z-pinch hypothesis. The shape-similarity claim has some visual force. However, Lascaux is ~17,000 years old (the host says 177,000 — clearly a transcription error), and mainstream archaeology and plasma physics both reject the inference: the morphological similarity is selective and the implied cosmic event would have left other physical evidence (e.g., paleomagnetic, isotopic) that hasn't been found.

claims.rationale: The SAFIRE Project is real and Aureon Energy in Ontario did publicly demonstrate a plasma device producing some of the listed phenomena (plasma double layers, anomalous heating). The transmutation claim, however, is the same kind of claim made by cold-fusion researchers and has not been replicated outside the project; Dr. Lyle Morgan, the only credentialed plasma physicist on the team, publicly stated the work is amateur/fraudulent (the host quotes him later). Self-organized plasma double layers are real plasma physics and well-known — they don't make a star and don't validate the Electric Sun thesis.

claims.text: Quantum entanglement does demonstrate non-local correlations between particles regardless of distance — the 2022 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Aspect, Clauser, and Zeilinger for closing the last loopholes in Bell-test experiments confirming that no local hidden-variable theory can explain the results.

claims.rationale: Bell's 1964 theorem, the 1972 Freedman/Clauser experiment, the 1981-82 Aspect experiments, the 1998 Zeilinger relativistic-locality test, and the 2015-2017 loophole-free Bell tests are all real, and Aspect/Clauser/Zeilinger did share the 2022 Nobel Prize for them. The 'particles communicate instantly' framing slightly oversells the result — entanglement does not allow superluminal signaling (no-communication theorem) — but the correlation result is genuinely non-local in the technical sense. Mostly accurate physics with one cautionary caveat the host elides.

claims.rationale: All four debunking points are well-sourced. (1) Venus has been recorded as a planet since at least 1500 BCE (Babylonian Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa), 1500+ years before Velikovsky's claimed catastrophe. (2) Thornhill's pre-impact Deep Impact predictions (electrical arcs, equipment failure, etc.) did not occur; the impact behavior was within standard expectations. (3) Thornhill's credentials are indeed an undergraduate physics start at Melbourne and a career at IBM, not a PhD or active research position. (4) Cherry-picking of mythological evidence is the standard methodological criticism of Velikovsky/Talbott from working classicists.

dependencies.rationale: Direct rebuttal of the Electric Universe core thesis.
Refutes the Velikovsky catastrophist chronology by citing pre-1500 BCE Venus observations.
Calls out the Talbott cosmic-wheel evidence as cherry-picked.

claims.text: Despite the EU theory being mostly wrong, Velikovsky was correct on a few specific predictions later borne out by space probes (Venus's high temperature, Jupiter's radio emissions, ancient American civilizations being older than then-believed) and the suppression of his work by the mid-20th-century scientific establishment is a documented case of scientific gatekeeping rather than self-correction.

claims.rationale: Velikovsky's hot-Venus prediction (Mariner 2, 1962) and Jupiter-as-radio-source prediction (1955 detection) are real correct predictions, though made for largely wrong reasons. The publisher boycott against Macmillan in 1950 is also a documented historical episode (the 'Velikovsky Affair' is a famous case study in sociology of science). The host's framing of the suppression as a generalized indictment of 'mainstream science' overstates: the same scientific community ultimately credited the correct predictions and the system did self-correct. Mixed accuracy.
